4 min read
Just now

--

Every year, thousands of US businesses get hit with a demand letter or federal complaint over something they never budgeted for: an inaccessible website. Retailers, restaurants, universities, hotels, streaming platforms — the industries vary, but the story repeats. A visitor using a screen reader, voice navigation, or keyboard-only input couldn’t complete a basic task, and that failure became a legal problem.
Here’s where these lawsuits come from, why the numbers keep climbing, and what a realistic compliance path looks like — without the scare tactics.
The Legal Backbone: What Actually Requires Accessibility
The Americans with Disabilities Act (ADA), passed in 1990, was written for physical spaces: ramps, doorways, signage. It never mentions websites. Yet courts have consistently ruled that Title III of the ADA, which covers “places of public accommodation,” extends to the digital storefronts and services tied to those businesses.
That interpretation was built case by case:

• Courts have repeatedly found that a company’s website is close enough to its physical operations (or is itself the primary point of service) to fall under Title III.

• Judges evaluating these cases have leaned on the Web Content Accessibility Guidelines (WCAG) as the practical yardstick for “accessible,” even though the ADA…
